stories.title: Seattle Chamber Music Society Plans to Build Concert Hall Inside US Bank Center
stories.excerpt: Seattle Chamber Music Society (SCMS) announced plans to build a new Center for Chamber Music in downtown Seattle—the city’s first permanent venue designed exclusively for chamber music performance, education, recording, and community engagement. Located within the newly refreshed U.S. Bank Center building on 6th & Union at the heart of Seattle’s arts ecosystem, the new Center for Chamber Music will house a 271-seat concert hall as well as rehearsal space, a hospitality suite, and the SCMS offices. Construction is set to begin in the winter of 2026 with an opening date planned for early 2028. The contractor for the project is Sellen Construction. The new Center for Chamber Music will become Seattle Chamber Music Society’s primary concert venue, offering greater flexibility for both festivals and year-round programming. SCMS will also continue to perform in other venues around Seattle and the greater Puget Sound region, including Benaroya Hall and Town Hall Seattle. The post Seattle Chamber Music Society Plans to Build Concert Hall Inside US Bank Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Seattle Chamber Music Society building 271-seat concert hall in U.S. Bank Center downtown
Construction winter 2026, opening early 2028; Sellen Construction as general contractor
Anchors arts ecosystem in downtown Seattle but no direct multifamily demand signal
stories.summary: The Seattle Chamber Music Society is developing a new 271-seat concert hall and office space within the U.S. Bank Center at 6th & Union in downtown Seattle, with construction starting winter 2026 and opening in early 2028. Sellen Construction is the general contractor. The project will consolidate the organization's venues and programming in the city's arts district.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. While the project reinforces downtown Seattle's cultural amenities and may support tertiary retail/hospitality demand near the site, it does not signal new employment, population migration, or residential demand drivers material to apartment underwriting.

stories.title: Prime Residential Pays $51M for Miracle Mile Apartments
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap closed the sale of Palm Court Apartments, a 132-unit multifamily property in the Miracle Mile neighborhood of Los Angeles. The asset traded for $51.25 million, or $388,258 per unit. “We created a competitive bidding environment, and the multiple offers we received underscore the continued demand for core Los Angeles commercial real estate,” said Jeff Louks, executive managing director investments in Marcus & Millichap’s Encino office. Louks, Gayle Factor, and Elliot Sabag of Marcus & Millichap represented the seller, a private investor. Matt Ziegler of Marcus & Millichap, along with Louks, Factor and Sabag, procured the buyer, Prime Residential. “The property’s extensive recent upgrades have modernized the community and enhanced its appeal to residents in one of Los Angeles’ most sought-after neighborhoods,” said Ziegler. Built in 1988 and located at 740 S Burnside Ave. near Wilshire Boulevard, the property gives residents immediate access to Museum Row, anchored by the Los Angeles County Museum of Art, and The Grove shopping mall. The post Prime Residential Pays $51M for Miracle Mile Apartments appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Prime Residential acquired 132-unit Palm Court in Miracle Mile, Los Angeles for $51.25M ($388k/unit)
Recently upgraded property near Museum Row, Grove shopping, Metro's Wilshire-Fairfax station
Multifamily investment sales in L.A. up ~25% YoY; L.A. accounts for 20%+ of primary market apartment trades
stories.summary: Prime Residential closed a $51.25 million acquisition of the 132-unit Palm Court Apartments in Los Angeles's Miracle Mile neighborhood, paying $388,258 per unit. The 1988-built property, which recently underwent extensive upgrades, benefits from proximity to Museum Row, The Grove, and the new Metro station at Wilshire and Fairfax. Marcus & Millichap brokered the off-market transaction in a competitive bidding environment.
stories.impact: L.A. multifamily investment velocity is accelerating sharply (25% YoY) and the metro now captures >20% of all primary-market apartment trades—signaling strong capital availability and buyer appetite. Miracle Mile's proximity to transit and retail makes it a stable value-add market; the speed and competition for this recently-renovated asset suggests limited supply of move-in-ready product and strong investor confidence in L.A. rents. This validates continued development focus on established L.A. submarkets with anchoring retail and transit.

stories.title: Tree Street Group Closes on Financing for Chicago’s First Geothermal Multifamily
stories.excerpt: Tree Street Group, together with development partners Magellan Development Group and Mark Goodman & Associates, has closed on construction financing for 410 N. Elizabeth, a 33-story high-rise in Chicago’s Fulton Market neighborhood. It will be Chicago’s first all-electric, geothermal multifamily high-rise and represents the first phase of a two-tower, mixed-use development. Financing included a senior loan from Bank OZK and junior capital from Beach Point Capital and Circle Property Partners. 410 N. Elizabeth will feature 38 studios, 87 one-bed, 256 two-bed and two three-bed apartments. The geothermal design and associated energy savings at 410 N. Elizabeth unlocked additional capital for the building, provided by Infinite Energy Partners, which will own and operate the pioneering system. This system, along with other key design features, will reduce energy usage by an estimated 60%, enabling the building to be Chicago’s first fully electric multifamily high-rise. Archeo Design Studio is serving as the architect of record for the development. Clark Construction Group is serving as the project’s general contractor. The conversations behind tomorrow’s apartment deals happen at Connect Apartments on September 22 in LA. Connect with the capital sources, owners, developers, and brokers driving multifamily investment. Learn more and reserve your seat at www.connectapts2026.com. The post Tree Street Group Closes on Financing for Chicago’s First Geothermal Multifamily appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Tree Street Group closed construction financing for 410 N. Elizabeth, 33-story, 383-unit high-rise in Chicago Fulton Market
Senior loan Bank OZK; junior capital from Beach Point Capital and Circle Property Partners; Infinite Energy Partners financing geothermal system
All-electric, geothermal design cuts energy 60%; unlocked additional capital and differentiated the development
stories.summary: Tree Street Group, Magellan Development Group, and Mark Goodman & Associates closed construction financing for 410 N. Elizabeth, a 33-story, 383-unit high-rise in Chicago's Fulton Market. The deal featured a senior loan from Bank OZK and junior capital from Beach Point Capital and Circle Property Partners. The project will be Chicago's first all-electric, geothermal multifamily tower, with a 60% energy reduction enabled by a system owned and operated by Infinite Energy Partners—a structure that unlocked additional capital.
stories.impact: Geothermal/all-electric design attracted dedicated capital (Infinite Energy Partners) as a direct equity source, materially improving the project's feasibility and potentially reducing cost of capital. In Fulton Market, this may set expectations for electrification and sustainability in competing developments, raising the bar for new entrants. The financing structure (separating energy system ownership) is a replicable model that unlocks ESG-driven capital for other urban infill projects and reduces developer reliance on traditional construction lending alone.

stories.title: M&T Proves Refi to Camber Realty JV on Manufacturing/R&D Portfolio
stories.excerpt: Cushman & Wakefield arranged a $40.8-million refinancing for a two-building manufacturing and R&D portfolio at 299 and 301 Ballardvale St. in Wilmington, MA. The Equity, Debt & Structured Finance team of Rob Borden, Heather Brown, Rehan Munsiff and Nolan Alexander represented the borrower in securing the refi from M&T Bank Totaling 200,750 square feet, the properties were enhanced and fully stabilized by ownership, a joint venture between Camber Development and GEM Realty Capital, over the past 12 months. The JV increased occupancy from 72% to 100% across a diversified tenant roster. “This transaction showcases a high-quality portfolio where the borrower created significant value through a disciplined lease-up and stabilization strategy,” said Borden. “The assets generated strong interest from a diverse pool of capital sources, resulting in a highly competitive financing process, and the outcome a testament to the successful execution of Camber and GEM.” The post M&T Proves Refi to Camber Realty JV on Manufacturing/R&D Portfolio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: M&T Bank refi'd $40.8M for two-building, 200,750-SF manufacturing/R&D portfolio in Wilmington, MA
JV (Camber Development & GEM Realty Capital) stabilized occupancy from 72% to 100% in 12 months
Competitive financing environment; strong sponsorship execution attracted diverse capital sources
stories.summary: Cushman & Wakefield arranged a $40.8 million refinancing from M&T Bank for a two-building, 200,750-square-foot manufacturing and R&D portfolio at 299 and 301 Ballardvale Street in Wilmington, MA. The joint venture between Camber Development and GEM Realty Capital stabilized the assets by raising occupancy from 72% to 100% over 12 months, which generated competitive interest from multiple lenders.
stories.impact: Not directly material to multifamily development. This is an industrial/R&D refi in a secondary market; however, it signals strong capital markets liquidity and lender appetite for operational value-add plays. The rapid occupancy recovery and financing success may indicate robust demand for flex industrial and R&D space in the Boston suburbs—a potential signal of tech/life-sciences job growth that could underpin residential demand in nearby markets like Cambridge or Somerville, but the nexus is indirect.

stories.title: Apartment Complex Sells for $51M in Los Angeles’ Miracle Mile
stories.excerpt: Prime Residential acquired the 132-unit complex in the Miracle Mile neighborhood for $51.3 million. An LLC tied to Studio City-based Harrison Properties sold the property at 740 South Burnside Avenue, named Palm Court Apartments, for approximately $388,000 per unit. Marcus & Millichap’s Jeff Louks, Gayle Factor and Elliot Sabag represented the seller, while Matt Ziegler procured the buyer. Ziegler said the seller recently completed extensive upgrades to the property. Palm Court is near Museum Row on Wilshire Boulevard, as well as the Grove shopping complex, the Original Farmers Market, and Metro’s new station at Wilshire and Fairfax Avenue. It was completed in 1988 with 48 one-bedroom apartments, 72 two-bedrooms and 12 three-bedrooms. It also features a pool and spa, a fitness center, saunas and a rooftop sundeck. The acquisition comes as multifamily investment activity picks up in L.A. Investment sales increased roughly 25 percent during the 12-month period ending in March, with L.A. accounting for more than 20 percent of all apartment trades across primary U.S. markets, according to Marcus & Millichap’s most recent market report. However, the city’s Measure ULA transfer tax continues to weigh on larger deals, as transactions above $10 million during the same period were down 50 percent compared to the year before the measure took effect in 2023. L.A.’s multifamily vacancy rate held at 4.8 percent at the start of 2026, while average effective rents increased 1.4 percent since the same period in 2025 to $2,853 per month. Marcus & Millichap forecasts vacancy will reach 5 percent by the end of this year as 8,500 units are added to the market. Prime Residential has owned the landmark Park La Brea community just north of Miracle Mile since 1995. It’s said to be the largest apartment complex west of the Mississippi River. It includes 4,249 rent-controlled units in 18 high-rise towers and 175 garden-style buildings on 144 acres. Gregory Cornfield can be reache
bullets: Prime Residential acquired 132-unit Palm Court, Miracle Mile, Los Angeles for $51.3M ($388k/unit)
Recent property upgrades; near Museum Row, Grove, Farmers Market, new Metro Wilshire-Fairfax station
L.A. multifamily sales volume +25% YoY; metro represents 20%+ of primary U.S. apartment trades
stories.summary: Prime Residential completed acquisition of the 132-unit Palm Court Apartments in Miracle Mile for $51.3 million, or approximately $388,000 per unit. The 1988-built property recently underwent extensive upgrades and is well-positioned near Museum Row, The Grove shopping complex, Original Farmers Market, and the newly opened Metro station at Wilshire and Fairfax. Marcus & Millichap brokered the transaction.
stories.impact: Duplicate of article 2 with slightly different reporting; same market signal applies. Strong L.A. investment velocity (+25% YoY) and capital concentration (20%+ of primary market trades) confirm robust buyer appetite and limited stabilized supply, particularly for recently upgraded assets. Developer opportunity to acquire and upgrade value-add properties or develop infill in Miracle Mile and adjacent submarkets benefiting from Museum Row anchoring and new transit access.

stories.title: Columbia University Acquires Student Housing in Morningside Heights for $122M
stories.excerpt: Columbia University is adding a new student housing project to its dormitory portfolio in northern Manhattan. Sam Charney’s Charney Companies sold its more than 300-unit project at 99 Claremont Avenue in Morningside Heights to the Ivy League university for $122 million, a source close to the deal confirmed. The Real Deal first reported the deal. Charney, along with Criterion Real Estate Capital, acquired the 80,000-square-foot building, which was originally a smaller 82-unit dorm next to Riverside Church, from an affiliate of the church for $38 million in 2024, with plans to convert it into luxury student housing. The joint venture secured $55 million of acquisition financing from Madison Realty Capital to purchase and redevelop the historic property, then known as McGiffert Hall, as Commercial Observer previously reported. “The opportunity to restore a historic, irreplaceable 92-year-old building with all the beauty that 99 Claremont offers while providing critically needed best-in-class student housing to New York City is a tremendous one,” Sam Charney said in 2024 following the acquisition. The finished project will be Columbia’s first residence hall dedicated to students in its School of General Studies (GS), according to a press release from Columbia in March. The dormitory will open in the fall. “This milestone reflects our commitment to meeting one of the most significant needs within our student population: accessible, community-centered housing designed specifically for nontraditional undergraduate students,” Lisa Rosen-Metsch, dean of Columbia’s School of General Studies, wrote in the March release. “Its close proximity to Morningside campus will reduce commute times and increase opportunities for students to deepen their engagement with one another, essential for the community building that is a hallmark of the GS experience.” Spokespeople for Charney and Columbia did not immediately respond to requests for comment. In addition to housing more than 300 st
bullets: Columbia University acquired 99 Claremont Avenue (300+ units) in Morningside Heights, Manhattan for $122M
Charney Companies and Criterion developed 80,000-SF conversion of historic McGiffert Hall; financed by Madison Realty Capital
Exit to university 12 months after acquisition; signals strong student housing demand and institutional appetite for Manhattan supply
stories.summary: Columbia University purchased 99 Claremont Avenue in Morningside Heights, a 300+-unit student housing project, from Charney Companies and Criterion Real Estate Capital for $122 million. Charney and Criterion had acquired the historic 82-unit McGiffert Hall building from a Riverside Church affiliate for $38 million in 2024, secured $55 million in acquisition financing from Madison Realty Capital, and converted it into luxury student housing. The rapid sale to the university represents a strong exit for the developers.
stories.impact: Institutional investor (Columbia) deploying capital to control on-campus student housing supply signals acute demand for quality student beds in Manhattan's constrained market. The 12-month turnaround from acquisition to major renovation and institutional sale indicates profitable value-add economics in NYC student housing. Developer takeaway: student housing in university neighborhoods with limited supply and strong enrollment growth can attract institutional capital at attractive multiples; however, regulatory/institutional buyer involvement may cap exit multiples compared to private-market comps.

stories.title: Miami design board swoons over OKO Lilli, 13th Floor’s Nobu condo towers
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s second condo tower in Edgewater and 13th Floor Investments’ proposal for a Nobu-branded residential building next to a historic Brickell church are advancing.  The Miami Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of both developments on Wednesday, with most board members gushing over the designs of what would be the latest luxury tower additions to the city’s quickly evolving skyline. One board member voted no on Doronin’s planned building, citing issues with some of the design.  Miami-based OKO Group, led by Doronin, plans the 53-story, 117-unit project, called OKO Lilli Tower, on the half-acre lot at 717 Northeast […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: OKO Group (Vlad Doronin) approved for 53-story, 117-unit OKO Lilli Tower in Edgewater, Miami, fronting Biscayne Bay
13th Floor Investments approved for 75-story, 321-unit Nobu-branded condo at 619 Brickell near historic church
Both projects cleared Miami UDRB; final approval with planning director; luxury condo market continuing momentum in Miami
stories.summary: Miami's Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of two luxury condo developments: OKO Group's 53-story, 117-unit OKO Lilli Tower in Edgewater (fronting Biscayne Bay with 350-foot public baywalk) and 13th Floor Investments' 75-story, 321-unit Nobu-branded tower at 619 Brickell (near First Miami Presbyterian Church). Both projects advanced with high board praise, though one board member voted no on OKO Lilli. Final approval rests with the city's planning and zoning director.
stories.impact: Luxury for-sale condo entitlements continue to advance in Miami, signaling developer and capital confidence in the high-end market despite prior overbuilding concerns. However, the projects are condo-focused, not rental multifamily—less relevant to apartment developers. Of note: Brickell's continued density and institutional investment (church preservation + mixed-use development) may validate rental multifamily upside in the submarket, though these specific projects won't compete directly with rental supply. Monitor actual absorption and pricing of these deliveries to assess market saturation.

stories.title: CRE Comes Together at MADE Bush Terminal for Cocktails and an Exciting New Development
stories.excerpt: On a humid and rainy July night in Sunset Park, Brooklyn, people from throughout the commercial real estate industry escaped the elements at the new mixed-use complex MADE Bush Terminal, which hosted Commercial Observer’s annual summer networking mixer. Amid a series of massive square wooden columns conveying the complex’s industrial feel, attendees networked, enjoyed drinks and small bites, and heard from some of MADE’s pioneering tenants on the benefits of taking space at the complex, which, in addition to sites like a brewery and a new 1,000-capacity music venue, will mostly host companies with light industrial uses. Bridget Chansakul, assistant vice president at the New York City Economic Development Corporation (NYCEDC), handles leasing for the development, and was excited for people to see the goings-on at Building A, where the event took place. While several tenants are already in place in that building, as well as in the project’s Building B, the full development and unveiling of MADE Bush Terminal, which will eventually be a five-building complex, will occur slowly over the next few years. “We’re giving a sneak peek tonight, but it’s just the beginning of what’s happening here in Sunset Park,” said Chansakul. “We’re getting ready to announce a new brewery coming to the site and we’re going to be unveiling a new building, so it’s just the beginning of an exciting and much larger project.” After brief introductions from Brian Pascus, a finance reporter at Commercial Observer, and Jennifer Brown, senior vice president for the Sunset Park district at NYCEDC, attendees were treated to a brief discussion for a deeper introduction to MADE Bush Terminal. Waverly Neer, vice president of asset management at NYCEDC, held a short panel discussion with two of the building’s earliest tenants: lighting manufacturer Stickbulb and design studio Pelle. Pelle, run by husband and wife architect team Jean and Oliver Pelle, moved into Building
bullets: MADE Bush Terminal mixed-use complex in Sunset Park, Brooklyn now leasing; hosts brewery, 1,000-seat music venue, light industrial
Brooklyn industrial/creative market sustaining tenant interest and mixed-use programming; event hosted industry networking
Building A occupied; NYCEDC leading leasing; light industrial and food/beverage mixed-use continue to activate industrial sites
stories.summary: MADE Bush Terminal, a new mixed-use complex in Sunset Park, Brooklyn, hosted Commercial Observer's summer networking event, showcasing its industrial-aesthetic space and tenant mix including a brewery, 1,000-capacity music venue, light industrial operators, and other creative tenants. Building A is already leasing and occupied. Bridget Chansakul at NYCEDC handles leasing for the development.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily development. MADE Bush Terminal is primarily industrial/creative/food-service mixed-use, not residential. However, the project's success in attracting tenants to Sunset Park and its mixed-use programming (venue, retail, light industrial) reinforces the submarket's evolution from pure manufacturing to live-work-play—potentially supporting higher density residential demand if rents and neighborhood stability improve. Developer should monitor whether MADE's activation drives population in-migration or residential rents in surrounding Sunset Park/Brooklyn neighborhoods.

stories.title: Palm Beach County nixes proposed Project Tango data center
stories.excerpt: Plans for a proposed data center in Palm Beach County hit a major roadblock Wednesday after county commissioners rejected a zoning application that would have allowed the project’s development.  At the end of a 12-hour hearing, officials voted 5-1 to deny an amended zoning application that would have permitted for a 1 million-square-foot data center at 20125 State Road 80/Southern Boulevard, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The project also would have included 2.3 million square feet of warehouses and 216,000 square feet for minor utility use.  Plans for Central Park Commerce Center, also known as Project Tango, were filed […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Palm Beach County commissioners rejected zoning application for Project Tango data center (1M SF) and warehouse complex (2.3M SF)
Vote 5-1 to deny; community opposition focused on infrastructure and environmental impacts; resubmission permitted
Large employer and industrial-zoning setback in rural South Florida; data center stalled, warehouse portion already approved
stories.summary: Palm Beach County commissioners voted 5-1 to reject a zoning amendment that would have allowed PBA Holdings and affiliates to develop Project Tango (Central Park Commerce Center), a 1 million-square-foot data center and 2.3 million-square-foot warehouse complex on State Road 80. The rejection followed a 12-hour hearing with community opposition citing infrastructure and environmental concerns. PBA Holdings had already secured approval for 2 million square feet of warehouses and can resubmit; litigation between PBA Holdings and co-applicant WPB Logistics Owner LLC is ongoing.
stories.impact: The data center denial removes a potential major employer anchor in rural Palm Beach County, reducing upside for residential infill and rental growth in the broader exurban market. However, the warehouse component remains approved, limiting the full employment hit. Broader signal: rural Florida county resistance to large-scale industrial development (data centers, warehouses) is intensifying due to infrastructure strain and community opposition—developers should expect longer entitlement timelines, higher mitigation costs, and tighter density caps. Multifamily developers relying on significant new job centers in exurban South Florida should hedge against permitting delays and community pushback.

stories.title: Stetson’s Law School, Bromley Partnering on Tampa Mixed-Use Venture
stories.excerpt: Stetson University plans to modernize the Stetson University College of Law’s Tampa Law Center and create a mixed-use space in a currently underutilized area with the help of Bromley Companies. Bromley hopes to maintain Stetson Law’s presence but add hotel rooms, apartments, restaurants and more. On the 6.5-acre site, Bromley envisions 664 multifamily units, 440,000 square feet of office space, 37,450 square feet of retail space and 300 hotel keys The developer is aiming for a late 2027 groundbreaking. The redevelopment will occur in phases, with no disruption to Stetson’s classes or operation on the property. The university filed a rezoning application to make way for the project, which will require approval from Tampa City Council. The University will retain ownership of the Stetson Law Center and have permanent access to use of the hotel and parking garage. The post Stetson’s Law School, Bromley Partnering on Tampa Mixed-Use Venture appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Stetson University and Bromley Companies planning 6.5-acre mixed-use development: 664 apartments, 440k SF office, 37.5k SF retail, 300 hotel keys
Late 2027 groundbreaking; phased development; university retains Law Center and permanent access to hotel/parking
Requires Tampa City Council rezoning; phases designed to preserve operations and university mission
stories.summary: Stetson University and Bromley Companies are partnering to redevelop the university's 6.5-acre Tampa Law Center site into a mixed-use complex comprising 664 apartments, 440,000 square feet of office space, 37,450 square feet of retail, and 300 hotel rooms. The project targets late 2027 groundbreaking and will be developed in phases with no disruption to Stetson's law school operations. The university will retain ownership of the Law Center and permanent access to hotel and parking facilities. Rezoning approval from Tampa City Council is pending.
stories.impact: Bromley's 664-unit development in Tampa signals developer confidence in the university-anchored mixed-use submarkets and adaptive reuse of institutional sites. The scale and University partnership reduce entitlement and financing risk. For Tampa market: this project validates infill multifamily demand near educational institutions and downtown connectivity. Developers should track: (1) absorption and rents post-delivery to gauge institutional-anchored submarket dynamics, (2) zoning precedent for future university partnerships, and (3) hotel-apartment-office stacking in Tampa to assess rental demand elasticity.

stories.title: Logistics Firm Highline Commerce Grows to 60K SF at Industry City, Thanks to Robots
stories.excerpt: Third-party logistics provider Highline Commerce is expanding its industrial footprint at Brooklyn’s Industry City for the second time in four years, Commercial Observer has learned. Highline’s lease renewal at 67 34th Street in Brooklyn’s Sunset Park neighborhood tacked on another 18,000 square feet to its warehouse space. The five-year deal rounds out the company’s total footprint at the mixed-use commercial campus to 60,000 square feet. The Brooklyn-based logistics company specializes in fulfilling orders for early-stage e-commerce brands, according to its website. Highline credits its growth, in part, to neighborly robots. The company has teamed up with fellow Industry City tenant Ultra Robotics to install “humanoid robots” that are fulfilling up to 30 percent of Highline’s clients’ orders, according to an Industry City spokesperson. The 60,000-square-foot flexible industrial space will remain largely dedicated to fulfillment operations, along with some office space and social rooms. Highline Commerce negotiated the deal directly with Industry City’s Jeff Fein and Brad Blum, who represent the landlord in-house. Asking rent was $28 per square foot. Highline arrived at Industry City from Manhattan’s Chelsea neighborhood in 2022. During its initial four-year lease, the company’s warehouse expanded from 27,000 square feet to 42,000 square feet. Highline plans to remain at Industry City as long as it can keep growing. “New York is in the middle of an e-commerce golden age, and brands are launching faster than the logistics world can support them,” Richard Hurley, founder and CEO of Highline, said in a statement. “Serving them doesn’t just take a village, it takes a place like Industry City, where on-site manufacturing, printing, fulfillment and the robots that run it all operate within feet of one another.” Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Highline Commerce expanded to 60,000 SF at Industry City, Brooklyn (second expansion in 4 years, +18k SF in latest lease)
E-commerce fulfillment tenant using humanoid robots (Ultra Robotics) to automate 30% of order fulfillment
Industrial logistics and automation are driving flex-space density and operational efficiency in Brooklyn
stories.summary: Third-party logistics provider Highline Commerce renewed and expanded its lease at Industry City in Brooklyn's Sunset Park, growing its footprint to 60,000 square feet—the second expansion in four years. The five-year renewal added 18,000 square feet. Highline has partnered with fellow tenant Ultra Robotics to deploy humanoid robots handling up to 30% of client orders, driving efficiency and growth without proportional real-estate expansion.
stories.impact: Automation in e-commerce logistics is reducing labor-intensity per square foot of industrial space, which may suppress demand for worker housing near industrial hubs like Sunset Park. Conversely, if robots free capital and allow e-commerce firms to scale faster, they could indirectly drive high-wage tech and operations jobs that anchor residential demand. The successful 60k-SF lease renewal at Industry City also validates mixed-use (industrial + creative + food) as a stable industrial-submarket strategy, potentially supporting adjacent residential development. Monitor whether logistics automation flattens or accelerates Brooklyn industrial-adjacent residential absorption.

stories.title: Bakery Shop Cafe Aroma to Open New Spot on the Upper East Side
stories.excerpt: Time to rise and grind, Upper East Siders, as a new coffee shop and bakery is set to open at Avenues Equity Partners’ 1410 Madison Avenue in Carnegie Hill. Family-owned coffee shop and bakery Cafe Aroma has taken 2,200 square feet of retail space at the base of the five-story residential building, according to landlord broker Meridian Capital Group. The asking rent and the length of the lease were not disclosed. The average asking rent for retail space on the Upper East Side was $252 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to data from CBRE. Meridian’s Jacob Mayer represented the landlord in this deal, while Shai Baradarian and Tamir Baradarian of eRealty Advisors represented Cafe Aroma. The Baradarians did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. “1410 Madison Avenue is a rare turnkey restaurant space in a high-traffic Upper East Side location, and it generated strong interest from operators looking to be near Mount Sinai and Carnegie Hill’s dense residential base,” Mayer said in a statement. “Cafe Aroma is a great fit for the space, and we’re pleased to have completed a lease that positions both the landlord and the tenant for long-term success in the corridor.” This will be Cafe Aroma’s sixth Manhattan location and its first on the Upper East Side. Avenues Equity Partners acquired 1410 Madison Avenue and 1412 Madison Avenue for $6.3 million in January 2025, according to property records. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Cafe Aroma (family-owned coffee/bakery) leasing 2,200 SF at Avenues Equity Partners' 1410 Madison Avenue, Carnegie Hill, Manhattan
Upper East Side retail avg asking rent $252/SF in Q2 2026; space positioned near Mount Sinai hospital and dense residential
Ground-floor retail capture in residential building; strong neighborhood anchors support retail tenancy
stories.summary: Cafe Aroma, a family-owned coffee shop and bakery, leased 2,200 square feet of ground-floor retail at 1410 Madison Avenue in Carnegie Hill (Manhattan's Upper East Side). The location, a five-story residential building owned by Avenues Equity Partners, benefits from proximity to Mount Sinai hospital and the dense residential base. Upper East Side retail was averaging $252 per square foot in Q2 2026. Meridian Capital Group represented the landlord; eRealty Advisors represented Cafe Aroma.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. Ground-floor retail tenancy in a residential building is supporting infrastructure, not a demand signal. However, the successful lease at $252/SF in a stabilized residential neighborhood confirms continued Upper East Side neighborhood stability and retail appeal, which validates the underlying rental market strength. Developers with Upper East Side multifamily assets should note sustained retail demand and low vacancy—ground-floor activation remains a value-add strategy for new residential development in the submarket.

stories.title: Musk’s companies prep $5M Seaholm office renovation
stories.excerpt: Downtown Austin’s Seaholm Power Plant, which was quietly occupied by several Elon Musk-affiliated companies earlier this year, is getting a $5.2 million facelift.  The planned renovations have been put in place by Wit Tech, a company with links to SpaceX, the firm that is pushing the construction of the Terafab semiconductor manufacturing plant in Grimes County. Musk’s companies occupy 35,000 square feet of space at 800 West Cesar Chavez Street, according to the Austin American Statesman. Musk is typically bombastic about his companies’ pursuits and ambitious, but has not yet publicly commented on the office space. He has given notes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Elon Musk-affiliated companies (via Wit Tech/SpaceX links) undertaking $5.2M renovation at Seaholm Power Plant, Austin; 35,000 SF occupied
Musk's Terafab semiconductor fab ($55B, 100M SF planned) in nearby Grimes County; Grimes approved tax breaks and tax increment reinvestment zone
Major employer anchoring rural Texas development; will drive population in-migration and residential demand across Austin metro and exurban corridor
stories.summary: Elon Musk-affiliated companies (linked to SpaceX via Wit Tech) are investing $5.2 million to renovate 35,000 square feet of office space at Seaholm Power Plant in downtown Austin. The renovation coincides with Musk's announced Terafab semiconductor manufacturing facility in Grimes County (north of Austin), a $55 billion project potentially reaching 100 million square feet. Grimes County Commissioners approved tax breaks and a tax increment reinvestment zone (TIRZ) for Terafab. Musk has also expanded land holdings in Bastrop (east of Austin) for an additional data-center facility.
stories.impact: Terafab semiconductor fab in Grimes County represents a generational employment and population anchor for the greater Austin metro. A potential $55B investment and 100M SF facility could drive 10,000–20,000+ jobs across manufacturing, engineering, construction, and services—massive in-migration tailwind for apartment demand across Austin proper, suburbs (Pflugerville, Round Rock, Williamson County), and exurban corridors. Developer opportunity: stage land acquisitions and multifamily projects along I-35 North and I-45 North corridors in Grimes/Brazos counties now, ahead of Terafab ramp and job influx. Tax incentives suggest regulatory support for industrial and potentially mixed-use development. Monitor Terafab construction pace and hiring announcements to calibrate absorption forecasts.

stories.title: Florida’s largest Live Local Act project is bold. Its ambitious developer is controversial
stories.excerpt: Pablo Castro came into South Florida real estate with a bang. Over the past two years, the Spanish developer, who has never built in the U.S. and until recently had zero name recognition here, proposed Florida’s largest known multifamily complex, bought the site, secured approval and stirred controversy: Castro faces lawsuits by three local industry players who say they were stiffed after playing a role in laying the groundwork for the project.  HueHub would consist of 4,040 units across seven 34-story towers on 12 acres in West Little River. It is planned under the state’s Live Local Act, which allows […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: HueHub by Spanish developer Pablo Castro: 4,040 units across 7 towers in West Little River, Miami, under Live Local Act
All units furnished and attainably priced (100% affordability exceeds Live Local's 40% requirement); free amenities (fitness, learning center, kitchens)
Developer facing lawsuits from local partners alleging non-payment; largest known Live Local Act project but execution risk high
stories.summary: Spanish developer Pablo Castro is planning HueHub, a 4,040-unit, seven-tower development in West Little River, Miami, under Florida's Live Local Act, which allows larger projects in exchange for affordable/workforce housing. All units will be furnished and attainably priced (exceeding Live Local's 40% affordable requirement), with free amenities. However, Castro faces multiple lawsuits from local industry players alleging unpaid partnership obligations. The project is Florida's largest known Live Local development but carries significant execution risk.
stories.impact: HueHub's 4,040-unit scale is unprecedented for Live Local Act projects and signals developer appetite to bundle large, affordably-priced rental portfolios under the regulatory umbrella. If delivered, it would be a material supply addition to Miami's workforce housing and could set occupancy/rent expectations for attainable multifamily. However, Castro's litigation (credibility, financial capacity questions) introduces execution risk; if project stalls or is delayed, it removes significant supply from market. Competing developers should: (1) monitor HueHub's financing and construction progress closely, (2) assess whether Live Local's regulatory incentives are sufficient to attract institutional capital to 4,000+ unit plays, and (3) prepare contingency if HueHub fails and the market absorbs a supply shortfall in West Little River/workforce housing segment.

stories.title: Faena Residences developers presell $30M penthouse for record price along Miami River
stories.excerpt: Shahab Karmely and Edgardo Defortuna’s joint venture presold a penthouse at their Faena-branded luxury condo development along the Miami River for $30 million, a record for the area on price as well as price per square foot.  Karmely’s KAR Properties has long planned to develop the waterfront site into the Rafael Viñoly-designed project. In 2022, Karmely brought on Defortuna’s Fortune International Group as a partner. Last year, they launched the Faena Residences.  A mystery buyer is under contract to buy a penthouse in the east tower that will span 8,200 square feet of interior space and also include 7,000 square […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Faena Residences (KAR Properties & Fortune International) presold penthouse at Miami River for $30M (~$3,700/SF), record for area
8,200 SF interior + 7,000 SF outdoor space; duplex with private rooftop, pool, wellness; pre-construction sale
Luxury condo pre-sales and record psf underscore Miami's continued high-end residential appeal and capital confidence
stories.summary: KAR Properties and Fortune International Group presold a penthouse at Faena Residences (Miami River, Rafael Viñoly design) for $30 million, breaking price and price-per-square-foot records for the neighborhood. The duplex spans 8,200 square feet of interior and 7,000 square feet of outdoor space, with private rooftop, pool, and wellness amenities. The presale at approximately $3,700 per square foot occurred before construction and follows a trend of high-priced penthouses locked in before groundbreaking.
stories.impact: Luxury condo pre-sales at record psf validate strong capital and ultra-high-net-worth buyer demand in Miami, but these are for-sale units, not rentals, so direct multifamily relevance is limited. However, the flight to high-end ownership in Miami may be signaling: (1) institutional and foreign capital seeking U.S. real estate safe havens (relevant to capital markets environment for all property types), and (2) continued bifurcation in Miami housing (ultra-luxury versus attainable/workforce)—creating runway for multifamily developers to focus on workforce/middle-income rentals if condo market continues to serve ultra-high-net-worth buyers. Monitor whether luxury condo velocity and pricing sustain or correct, as it may affect financing availability for rental projects.

stories.title: LA city’s top construction permits: Week ending July 11, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 The largest development to receive permits last week was for a single-family build in Encino, where plans were filed for 11,278 square feet of work across two permits at 3864 North Kim Lane. The project includes a new two-story single-family dwelling with an attached three-car garage and a pond. Job Filing Numbers: 25010-10000-05697, 25010-10000-05874, 25047-10000-01751 and more 📊 In Valley Glen, permits were issued for 8,765 square feet of construction across three permits at 14414-14418 1/2 West Tiara Street. The project includes building a new accessory dwelling unit, along with a three-story duplex. The work is estimated to cost […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: LA construction permits week ending July 11: largest single-family at 3864 North Kim Lane, Encino (11,278 SF two-story + garage)
Valley Glen: 8,765 SF permits for ADU + three-story duplex (~$880k estimated cost)
Bel Air: "The One" megamansion (Fashion Nova owner) filed $350k+ alteration permits; Van Nuys: $1.1M golf club addition
stories.summary: During the week ending July 11, 2026, Los Angeles issued building permits across multiple residential and commercial projects, with the largest single-family development in Encino (11,278 SF two-story with three-car garage and pond), a Valley Glen multi-family addition (ADU + duplex, $880,500 estimated cost), and alteration work at "The One" megamansion in Bel Air (75,856 SF interior and roof-deck modifications, $350,000+ estimated). Van Nuys permitted $1.1 million in commercial upgrades at a golf club.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development activity. Permits reflect single-family and small mixed-use/ADU projects, not production multifamily. However, the volume of single-family and ADU permits suggests persistent demand for owner-occupied housing in LA's affluent neighborhoods (Encino, Bel Air), which may constrain land availability for apartment development in those submarkets. Developers should note that smaller-lot, ADU-driven projects are capturing capital and entitlements in some LA neighborhoods—consider focusing multifamily development on core submarkets (Miracle Mile, Koreatown, arts districts) with lower single-family competition.
